Entity_Description: Quadra Island is an unincorporated community in the Strathcona Regional District off the east coast of Vancouver Island, British Columbia, and the largest of the Discovery Islands. As of the 2021 Census it had a population of about 2,700. Reached by a roughly ten-minute BC Ferries crossing of Discovery Passage from Campbell River, the island centres on Quathiaski Cove and Heriot Bay, is home to the We Wai Kai Nation at Cape Mudge, and features Rebecca Spit Marine Provincial Park. Its economy rests on tourism, aquaculture and forestry.

The largest of the Discovery Islands, Quadra stretches from the ferry landing at Quathiaski Cove north to Heriot Bay and south to the Cape Mudge Lighthouse, a scatter of waterfront cottages, rural acreages, and homes tucked into second-growth forest. {COMPANY_NAME} understands what serving an island community demands: reliable scheduling around the sailings, {ESTIMATE_TYPE} estimates, and {BUSINESS_TYPE} work built to withstand salt air and abundant Pacific rainfall. Whether you are out near Rebecca Spit or overlooking Discovery Passage, our team brings genuine local knowledge to every {PROPERTY_TYPE} we serve. Call {PHONE}.
Long_Description:
Quadra Island is not just another dot on a Vancouver Island service map. As the largest of the Discovery Islands, it sits across Discovery Passage from Campbell River, linked to the rest of the world by a roughly ten-minute BC Ferries sailing that lands at Quathiaski Cove. That single detail shapes almost everything about serving this community, and it is exactly why {COMPANY_NAME} takes Quadra seriously rather than treating it as an afterthought behind the mainland island coast.

The island has a distinct geography of its own. Quathiaski Cove is the working heart near the ferry, Heriot Bay on the east side is the jumping-off point for the onward ferry to Cortes Island, and the south end runs down to Cape Mudge, home to the We Wai Kai Nation, the Nuyumbalees Cultural Centre, and the historic Cape Mudge Lighthouse guarding the passage. In between sits Rebecca Spit Marine Provincial Park, a slender gravel bar reaching into Drew Harbour. Homes here range from waterfront cottages and view properties along the strait to rural acreages and cabins set back in the second-growth forest, a building stock that asks different things of a {SERVICE} provider than any subdivision on the drier east-coast plain.

The climate does not make it easier. Quadra shares the wet, mild coastal temperate rainforest weather of the region, with abundant Pacific rainfall and salt-laden air off Discovery Passage that works relentlessly on exteriors, finishes, and building systems. {COMPANY_NAME} plans {SERVICE} around those conditions instead of pretending an island in the Salish Sea behaves like an inland town.

The local economy leans on tourism, aquaculture, and forestry, with seasonal summer peaks as visitors arrive for kayaking, fishing, and the trails of the island's provincial parks. That rhythm affects both residential and commercial clients, from lodges and rentals near Heriot Bay to year-round homes around Quathiaski Cove. {COMPANY_NAME} coordinates {SERVICE_LIST} with {RESPONSE_TIME} response and {ESTIMATE_TYPE} estimates, scheduling crews and materials around ferry sailings so island jobs actually get finished on time. With roughly 2,700 residents plus a steady stream of visitors, Quadra Island rewards a {BUSINESS_TYPE} who knows the ground. Call {COMPANY_NAME} at {PHONE} or reach us at {EMAIL} to get started.
Local_Facts:
- Population: approximately 2,700 (2021 Census)
- Largest of the Discovery Islands, in the Strathcona Regional District, British Columbia
- Connected to Campbell River by a roughly 10-minute BC Ferries crossing of Discovery Passage, landing at Quathiaski Cove
- Heriot Bay on the east side is the ferry link onward to Cortes Island
- Cape Mudge is home to the We Wai Kai Nation, the Nuyumbalees Cultural Centre, and the historic Cape Mudge Lighthouse
- Rebecca Spit Marine Provincial Park reaches into Drew Harbour on the island's southeast shore
- Economy driven by tourism, aquaculture, and forestry, with strong seasonal summer peaks
